explanation: This property captures the requirement that after a power failure, the system (which implicitly includes its internal power source) must remain operational for 12 time units. We interpret 'operation' as the system not being off (i.e., "off" is false) after a power failure. Thus, whenever powerFailure becomes true, for the next 12 time units, the system must not turn off (off is false). This is expressed using a bounded globally-operator (G[0,N_DURATION]) after powerFailure, ensuring continued operation for 12 units after external power is lost. We use the LTL template (14) where bool_exp2 is set to 'powerFailure' and bool_exp3 to '! off'.

The requirement is that the system should operate for at least 12 time units after external power fails. This means that when the power failure occurs (represented by the 'powerFailure' atomic proposition), the system should remain operational for at least 12 time units after that failure. Since we do not have a direct atomic proposition indicating 'system operational,' but since the requirement is focused on power backup time after power failure, we can model this by requiring that there must be an interval of at least 12 units following any 'powerFailure' where the system is supported (implicitly by the internal power source). This is best represented by a timed guarantee after the event; thus, the template '(G (bool_exp2 -> (F[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3)))' fits: whenever bool_exp2 (powerFailure) happens, within N_DURATION (=12) time units, bool_exp3 ("system has internal power capability after external power failure"; here, we can conservatively model it as powerFailure since system support itself is not a direct AP). As such, the LTL template ensures that, after a power failure, operation continues for at least 12 time units, in accordance with the requirement. Since the atomic proposition only exists for power failure, we use it on the trigger side.

The requirement states that after the failure of the external power source (represented by 'powerFailure'), the system must continue to operate for 12 time units using an internal power source. None of the atomic propositions directly define 'system operation,' so we interpret continued operation as the absence of system shutoff ('off' = system is completely de-energized). Thus, after 'powerFailure,' it must not become 'off' for 12 time units, i.e., 'off' must not occur globally within 12 time units after 'powerFailure,' but 'off' may occur after that. This is modeled by using the template for "if condition, then property globally/certain interval": G(powerFailure -> ((G[0,12] (! off)) | (G (! off)))).

The requirement is that after a power failure (represented by 'powerFailure'), the system continues operating (by internal power) for at least 12 time units. Since there is no specific atomic proposition for internal power, we interpret ongoing operation as the absence of 'off' (system de-energized) for at least 12 steps after a power failure. Thus, globally, whenever a power failure occurs, the system must not transition to 'off' for the next 12 time units. Template 33 best captures this: it expresses that, whenever a power failure happens, 'off' must not become true during the following 12 time units, and that at some point within 13 time units, 'off' may occur or another event terminates this interval—but the main requirement is the prevention of 'off' for at least 12 units after the failure.

The requirement is that after a power failure ('powerFailure'), the system must remain in operation (i.e., not 'off') for at least 12 time units. This can be captured in LTL as: whenever a power failure occurs, then for the next 12 time units the system must stay on (not 'off'). We use '!off' to denote the system is operating. The template G (bool_exp1 -> (F[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3)) captures this: globally, after 'powerFailure', within the next 12 time units, '!off' (i.e., system operating) must occur. This verifies that the system is not immediately turned off and has enough stored power to remain operational.

The requirement states that after a failure of the external power source, the system must continue to operate (powered by an internal source) for at least 12 time units. To translate this, we identify the atomic proposition 'powerFailure' (indicates any power failure), which is triggered when the external power source fails. The system's 'operation' in the sense of being on/functional is inferred as staying 'not off' (since the only available atomic proposition regarding complete power loss is 'off', meaning the system is completely de-energized).

Therefore, we need to guarantee that after any occurrence of 'powerFailure', the system does not shut off (i.e., '!off') for at least 12 time units (N_DURATION = 12) following the failure. This specification is captured by the template:
14 - G (powerFailure -> (F[0,12] (!off))).

This states: "Globally, whenever a power failure occurs, it must be followed, within 12 time units, by the system remaining not off (i.e., system stays operational)." This template fits the requirement that the system, upon losing external power, continues operating on an internal source for at least the specified period.

The property states that whenever (globally) there is a failure of external power (i.e., 'powerFailure' becomes true), then the system must continue to operate for at least 12 time units. Here, 'off' represents the system being completely de-energized and not operating ('off' is true). So, after a 'powerFailure', it must not be the case that 'off' becomes true for at least N=12 time units. This is expressed using the template for bounded until: after power failure, it is globally not 'off' for [0,12] time units, or forever, which captures the requirement of 12 units of continued operation after external power fails.

The requirement specifies that when the external power source fails (represented as !powerConnected), the system's internal power source must ensure continued operation—meaning the system shall not be 'off'—for at least 12 time units after that event. Choosing template 14, we specify that globally (at all times), if an external power failure is detected (!powerConnected), then for the next 12 time units the system remains operational (!off). This LTL formulation directly encodes the required behavior.

The requirement states that after a power failure (represented by 'powerFailure'), the system must remain operational for at least 12 time units. There is no proposition directly indicating 'internal power source in use', but ensuring that the system is not 'off' after a power failure captures that it is still operating, presumably due to the internal power source. Thus, we use the template: Globally, if 'powerFailure' then (Globally for 12 time units, not off), ensuring that anytime a power failure occurs, the system cannot enter the off state within the next 12 time units. 'powerFailure' is used as the trigger (bool_exp1), and '! off' expresses the system must remain on (bool_exp3).

The requirement is that after a failure of external power (i.e., a 'powerFailure' occurs and the system is not connected to wall/external power, that is, '!powerConnected'), the system must be able to operate using its internal power source for at least 12 time units. This can be encoded as: whenever 'powerFailure & !powerConnected' happens, for the next 12 time units, the system continues to operate (which, in the absence of a dedicated 'internalPowerAvailable' or 'systemOperable' proposition, is assumed implicit by the absence of a further 'off' or failure event for now). The template that best captures this is: 'G ( (powerFailure & !powerConnected) -> (G[0,N_DURATION] (!off)) )'. Since there is no explicit indicator for 'system is still working', we assume the system remains operational as long as it is not 'off'. Hence, we use 'G (condition -> requirement for N time units)' and set N_DURATION=12. In the template system, this is captured by template 12.
